content: Hepatitis C virus (HCV) is an RNA flavivirus causing acute and chronic hepatitis. Transmission primarily percutaneous (injection drug use most common in developed countries); sexual and perinatal transmission less efficient. Acute infection often asymptomatic; 75-85% progress to chronic infection. Chronic HCV leads to cirrhosis in 10-20% over 20-30 years; hepatocellular carcinoma risk increases with cirrhosis. Extrahepatic manifestations: cryoglobulinemia, glomerulonephritis, porphyria cutanea tarda, lymphoma. Genotypes 1-7; genotype 1 most common globally. Unlike HBV, no vaccine exists. Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) revolutionized treatment with >95% sustained virologic response (SVR, considered cure) across all genotypes with 8-12 week oral regimens.

Pangenotypic DAA regimens (preferred, genotype testing not required): glecaprevir/pibrentasvir 3 tabs daily x8 weeks (treatment-naïve without cirrhosis) or x12 weeks (compensated cirrhosis); sofosbuvir/velpatasvir 1 tab daily x12 weeks for all genotypes. Decompensated cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B/C): sofosbuvir/velpatasvir + ribavirin x12 weeks or sofosbuvir/velpatasvir x24 weeks (protease inhibitors contraindicated). Prior treatment failure: tailored retreatment based on prior regimen, resistance-associated substitutions. HIV/HCV coinfection: same regimens as HCV monotherapy; check drug-drug interactions with ART. Post-SVR: monitor for HCC if cirrhosis present (SVR reduces but doesn't eliminate risk). No role for interferon-based therapy. Universal HCV screening recommended for adults 18-79 years.

HCV diagnosis algorithm: 1) Anti-HCV antibody screening (EIA); 2) If reactive, reflex to HCV RNA PCR to confirm active infection (antibody indicates exposure only - can be false positive or resolved infection). HCV RNA quantification establishes baseline viral load. Genotype testing needed only if using genotype-specific regimens. Liver disease staging: non-invasive methods preferred (FIB-4, APRI, FibroScan/elastography); biopsy rarely needed. ALT may be normal despite significant fibrosis. Spontaneous clearance occurs in 15-25% of acute infections (more likely symptomatic, IL28B CC genotype). Reinfection possible after SVR; ongoing risk reduction counseling essential. HCV core antigen assay alternative where RNA testing unavailable.
